channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: All New York Suffers in a Heat Wave. It’s Worse in Jamaica, Queens.
item.description: “Heat-vulnerable” parts of the city have few trees, little shade and limited access to air-conditioning. And the risk of death is also higher.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Maslin Nir and Christopher Maag
item.media:credit: Ryan Murphy for The New York Times
item.media:description: Because the fountains at the playground near his house were not working, Chris Rousseau took his daughter to a park further in Jamaica, Queens. Fortunately, the sprinklers were on.

item.title: See How Europe’s Heat Waves Melted the Alps’ Glaciers
item.description: The snowfall from last winter disappeared a month sooner than usual, after two early hot spells. Huge volumes of exposed ice are now starting to vanish.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong and Mira Rojanasakul
item.media:description: The Rhône Glacier in the Swiss Alps in 2025. The melting is already at levels typical of the end of summer.

item.title: A Sweltering Fourth of July
item.description: Also, Taylor’s wedding. Here’s the latest at the end of Thursday.
item.dc:creator: Evan Gorelick
item.media:credit: Carolina Herrera for The New York Times
item.media:description: Chinatown in Manhattan today.

item.title: F.B.I. Assigns Scores of Analysts to Examine Election Records in Georgia
item.description: The expansion of an investigation into Fulton County’s election office reflects President Trump’s desire to prove his baseless claims that the 2020 election in Georgia was rigged.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti, Alan Feuer, Dustin Volz and Richard Fausset
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: A memo says that the surge is part of a priority effort by Kash Patel, the director of the F.B.I.

item.title: Texas Governor Appoints Former Hard Right Critic to Key Post
item.description: Gov. Greg Abbott appointed a firebrand former opponent, Don Huffines, to be state comptroller, underscoring a G.O.P. push for unity ahead of the midterms.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Don Huffines at a forum featuring gubernatorial candidates running to unseat Gov. Greg Abbott in 2022.

item.title: Tucker Carlson, Who Broke With Trump, Plans to ‘Help Build a Third Party’
item.description: The influential conservative commentator’s relationship with the president and the G.O.P. fractured over the war with Iran. Now, he says he is charting a new course.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:description: Tucker Carlson, a former Trump ally, broke with the G.O.P. over the war with Iran.

item.title: David Hearn, Olympian Canoeist, Is Indicted After Arrest at Trump’s Reflecting Pool
item.description: David Hearn, a former canoeist, is accused of vandalizing the algae-challenged pool, which President Trump has said he would repair and beautify with fresh sealant. But the algae returned and the paint peeled.
item.dc:creator: Campbell Robertson and Alan Feuer
item.media:description: In 2000, David Hearn was one of America’s top competitive canoeists.

item.title: Trump-Linked Firm’s Fees for Organizing Freedom 250 Events Remain Secret
item.description: Event Strategies, Inc., a longtime vendor to President Trump’s campaigns, is organizing the Great American State Fair and the July 4 celebrations.
item.dc:creator: David A. Fahrenthold and Andrea Fuller
item.media:description: A company with deep ties to President Trump is organizing the Great American State Fair and the fireworks display planned for Saturday.

item.title: With Attacks on Crimea, Ukraine Hopes to Pressure Putin to End the War
item.description: Escalating drone strikes, fuel shortages and power cuts in the region Russia annexed are among the factors turning up the heat on President Vladimir V. Putin.
item.dc:creator: Paul Sonne and Nataliya Vasilyeva
item.media:description: Yevpatoriya, Crimea, last month. The Black Sea region, which Russia annexed from Ukraine in 2014, has become a flashpoint in the war.

item.title: Even as It Bombards Kyiv, Russia’s Wider Military Campaign Has Largely Stalled
item.description: Moscow can still inflict serious damage, but its forces are suffering higher casualties than their Ukrainian counterparts, analysts say.
item.dc:creator: Marc Santora
item.category: Center for Strategic and International Studies
item.media:credit: Brendan Hoffman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ukrainian soldiers on the battlefield earlier this year.

item.title: U.S. Officials Believed Israel Was Plotting to Kill Iranian Negotiators
item.description: Any Israeli attempt to kill Abbas Araghchi, the foreign minister, or Mohammad Ghalibaf, the Parliament speaker, would have derailed peace talks, American officials feared.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes and Farnaz Fassihi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Abbas Araghchi, left, with Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, traveled to Switzerland in June for a second in-person meeting with Vice President JD Vance and the American delegation.

item.title: Man Rescued 8 Days After Quake, a Ray of Joy in Stricken Venezuela
item.description: The 44-year-old security guard was pulled alive from a pancaked basement, offering a fleeting moment of hope amid a soaring death toll.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Sulbarán, Shawn Hubler and Genevieve Glatsky
item.media:description: Rescue workers attend to Hernán Alberto Gil Flores after he was pulled from the rubble eight days after he was trapped by twin earthquakes that struck Catia La Mar, Venezuela, on Thursday.

item.title: An Aerial View of Disaster at a Venezuela Housing Project
item.description: What one aerial image of a collapsed building tells us about the aftermath of an earthquake in La Guaira, Venezuela.
item.dc:creator: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times

item.title: Mamdani Is a Taylor Swift Fan. Here Are His Top 12 Songs.
item.description: Ahead of Taylor Swift’s wedding celebration in Manhattan, Mayor Zohran Mamdani revealed that he is a bigger fan of her music than previously known.
item.dc:creator: Emma G. Fitzsimmons
item.media:description: The wedding celebration of Taylor Swift and her fiancé Travis Kelce is set to take place on Friday at Madison Square Garden.

item.title: How, and Why, We’re Covering Taylor Swift’s (Maybe) Wedding
item.description: About two dozen reporters and editors from several desks are leading The Times’s coverage of the anticipated festivities in Manhattan this week.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:description: “America doesn’t have royal weddings,” said Emma Fitzsimmons, a New York Times reporter who is covering the expected nuptials of Travis Kelce and Taylor Swift. “But this is probably the closest thing.”

item.title: A ‘Muslims Only’ Swim Party and the Backlash That Followed
item.description: A Muslim mother’s plan for an Eid swim party near Dallas drew backlash, then threats that upended her quiet life.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman and Desiree Rios
item.media:description: Aminah Knight and her husband, Imam Muhammad Abdullah, pose for a portrait in Fort Worth.

item.title: Less Than 10 Percent of Eligible Children Have Trump Accounts
item.description: More than six million have been signed up for the investment accounts, which open for contributions July 4, but there are barriers to uptake.
item.dc:creator: Claire Cain Miller
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: Calling them Trump accounts has turned away some people, researchers said.

item.title: In Tumultuous Term, Chief Justice Roberts Took Charge of Unruly Supreme Court
item.description: The chief justice faced down the president, forged unlikely coalitions and achieved long-sought goals.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.category: New York Times v Sullivan (Supreme Court Decision)
item.media:description: Chief Justice John Roberts in Washington, D.C., last year.

item.title: Supreme Court Allows Reporter to Be Fined for Failing to Disclose Source
item.description: Catherine Herridge, a former Fox News reporter, was held in civil contempt by a lower court after she refused to reveal her sources for articles she wrote about a scientist who was investigated by the F.B.I.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.category: Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press
item.media:description: Catherine Herridge invoked First Amendment protections to try to avoid being forced to testify and identify her sources.

item.title: Black Soldiers In the Revolutionary War: A New England Journey
item.description: Across New England, a writer traveled down dirt paths and into historic homes to find the forgotten stories of the enslaved men who fought for freedom on two fronts.
item.dc:creator: Lizzie Skurnick and Lucy Lu
item.media:description: The history of abolition in New England tends to obscure the region’s equally robust history of slavery, which drastically shaped the fates of Black Americans during the Revolutionary War.

item.title: How to Make a Time Capsule That Will Last 250 Years
item.description: The America250 time capsule will hold dozens of historical treasures. Avoiding dampness and other dangers requires lots of engineering.
item.dc:creator: K. R. Callaway
item.category: National Institute of Standards and Technology
item.media:description: Sarah Reeves places items into a time capsule at National Institute of Standards and Technology in Gaithersburg, Md. The capsule will be buried in Philadelphia on July 4.

item.title: Amy Coney Barrett Is the Lightning Rod
item.description: The Supreme Court seesaw is swinging hard.
item.dc:creator: Emily Bazelon and David French
item.media:credit: Will Matsuda for The New York Times

item.title: Taylor Swift, What Are You Doing?
item.description: Why am I dreading the Swift-Kelce wedding?
item.dc:creator: Alice Bolin

item.title: Empire State Climbers Accused of Breaking Security Door to Get to Spire
item.description: Angela Nikolau and Ivan Beerkus, extreme climbers who have illegally scaled buildings around the world, were arrested after getting engaged atop the Manhattan landmark.
item.dc:creator: Miles G. Cohen and Hurubie Meko
item.media:credit: Jefferson Siegel for The New York Times
item.media:description: Angela Nikolau and Ivan Beerkus, who have illegally climbed buildings worldwide, were arrested after getting engaged atop the Empire State Building.
